content.problemStatement:
After many years of work, Chef has managed to open an art gallery showcasing all his pieces.

There are NNN pieces of art, arranged in a line from left to right.

The iii-th piece from the left has a beauty of AiA_iAi​.

Each AiA_iAi​ equals either 111 or 222.

A famous art critic is visiting the gallery, and Chef knows that he will move through the gallery as follows.

Initially, the critic will start off by viewing the first art piece. Then, if ppp denotes the index of the piece currently being viewed,


If Ap=1A_p = 1Ap​=1, the critic will move to piece p+1p+1p+1.
If Ap=2A_p = 2Ap​=2, the critic will be absorbed in the beauty of what he just saw, and so will move to piece p+2p+2p+2 without viewing piece p+1p+1p+1.
More generally, starting from p=1p = 1p=1, after viewing piece ppp the critic will move to piece p+App + A_pp+Ap​.

If p+Ap>Np + A_p \gt Np+Ap​>N, the critic will not view any more pieces.

For an arrangement of art pieces, let f(A)f(A)f(A) denote the number of them that the critic will view.

Chef wants f(A)f(A)f(A) to be as **large** as possible.

To achieve this, he can swap adjacent art pieces.

Functionally, this corresponds to choosing an index iii (1≤i<N1 \le i \lt N1≤i<N) and performing swap(Ai,Ai+1)\text{swap}(A_i, A_{i+1})swap(Ai​,Ai+1​).

Your task is to find the **minimum number** of such adjacent swaps needed to attain the maximum possible value of f(A)f(A)f(A).


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the answer: the minimum number of adjacent swaps needed to maximize f(A)f(A)f(A).
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: With the given arrangement, the critic will behave as follows:

Start at p=1p = 1p=1 and view the first piece.
Then, because Ap=2A_p = 2Ap​=2, move to p+2=3p+2 = 3p+2=3.
View the piece at p=3p = 3p=3.
Then, because Ap=2A_p = 2Ap​=2 and p+2>Np+2 \gt Np+2>N, no more pieces will be viewed.

So, f(A)=2f(A) = 2f(A)=2 for the initial arrangement.
It can be shown that no matter what Chef does, it's impossible to make the critic view all 333 pieces. So, the maximum possible f(A)f(A)f(A) equals 222, and no swaps are needed.
Test case 222: All values of AiA_iAi​ are the same, so swapping cannot change anything.
Test case 333: The initial arrangement has f(A)=3f(A) = 3f(A)=3, because the critic will view pieces 1,2,1, 2,1,2, and 444.
However, if Chef swaps the last two pieces to obtain the arrangement [1,2,1,1,2][1, 2, 1, 1, 2][1,2,1,1,2], the critic will view pieces 1,2,4,51, 2, 4, 51,2,4,5 which is better.
It's impossible for all 555 pieces to be viewed, so 444 is the best and one swap is needed to get there.
